titleStmt.title: Letter from Maria Edgeworth to
titleStmt.author: Maria Edgeworth
titleStmt.sponsor: Texas A & M University, University of Tennessee, Wake Forest University, Xavier University of Louisiana, Southern Methodist University
titleStmt.principal: Susan Egenolf, Meredith Hale, Hilary Havens, Jessica Richard, and Robin Runia

editionStmt.edition: First digital edition in TEI, date:

availability.p: Reproduced by courtesy of Bodleian Library
availability.licence: Distributed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0) Unported License

msDesc.head:
Letter from
Maria Edgeworth 1768-1849 71477273 62567029
to ref="./Personography.xml#RuxtM1">Margaret Ruxton, Black-Castle, 1746-1830

sourceDesc.bibl: In addition to our edited version, this letter was previously published in

editorialDecl.p: Edgeworth’s spelling and punctuation are retained in transcription, including non-standard spelling, line breaks, and hyphenation of words. The long s and ligatured forms are silently emended.

div.p:
My dearest Aunt - Thank God you
are well - If Sophy had any anxiety
about you at this time she would
be quite worn out. Thank God you
are well! for her sake, for your own, for
our own - You have done most wisely
and kindly to insist upon Sophy & Mar
garets not sitting up at night any more
with that poor sufferer, - for it is only wear-
ingwearing out their health to no purposes. All that
can be done has been done for and is
done her by Sophy's exertions and by
your tender providing care - Our own
Mrs. Billamore under my mothers
care and Harriets was not better attended
- This thought is always a comfort -
I wish her sufferings were at an end for
in her condition life must be dreadful
and she has been such a good creature
all her life that she cannot dread death.

I have no means to tell you of our-
selves - but that Lovell is come home and
will stay here some time - of which we are
very glad - Mr. King is to be here the 23d.
and as you may guess Lucy and all of us
are most anxious for his arrival & his
opinion - Lovell invited Emmeline &
one of her daughters to come, and she
answered that she was delighted with
the thoughts of re-visiting her old home
; but I do not think she will be able to come
now with Mr. King - but that she will
certainly come at a future time - He will
come by the steam packet & we shall hear
how that is as to accomodations &c

Honora will stay at Lough Glyn with
Mr. and Mrs. Strickland till about the
20th. of August & then I go for her & stay two
days & bring her back - She likes the Strick-
landStricklands much - Indeed they are highly me-
ritoriousmeritorious in their judicious & incessant
exertions for the relief of the poor & the im
-provementimprovement of the people in their neighbor-
hoodneighborhood . It is very extraordinary that in the
that part of the county of Monaghan to which
Mr. Strickland sent last week for flax seed
for the s poor tenants in his neighborhood
he found that there is plenty of every thing
- No distress felt - The distress Famine seems to
have been as capricious as the Malaria
in W passing over some spots and lighting &
settling upon others - We go on in our parish
without having recourse to public subscription
- The poor near us did not depend entirely on
the potatoes - they were accustomed to buy
meal and often meat - and tho they have
spent all their money & have to look for
the rent they are not starving - How we
shall look or do without the rent I do not
know - But Lovell is better paid than
almost any other landlord in this County

He is now a magistrate & will act
and is to meet the other magistrates of the Cy.
- here a at their own places frequently.
So much the better - The more he mixes with
the gemen of the country - He is really so
goodtempered & takes with such kindness
remonstrances against even his darling pur
suitspursuits taking him away too much that it is
almost impossible to find fault with him
- I wrote him a strong remonstrance & he seems
to like me the better for it and what is better
is now determined to do all we asked - His
school is going on exceedingly well.

Wednesday Eveg So far had I written my dearest Aunt when
yours and Sophy's letter arrived bringing
the news of poor faithful Molly Coffey's
death - I almost reproached myself for
what I had written at the moment I heard it
- Yet I cannot retract - She could not have en-
joyedenjoyed life and her death has been soothed
by the kindest attendance of the best of mistresses
and friends - and by the sense of her own
innocent useful life - I am glad that the
health of my dear & most valuable Sophy
is no longer to undergo such severe trial
- It must however be very melancholy
to you all to lose such a faithful attached
servant - I wish I was with you to cheer
you dearest friends - Pray beware dear
Aunt of those tempters new potatoes -

I must long to know what you mean
about Brougham - None of your newspaper
readers have seen any thing about it - Fanny
is going a hunting for it - I had a kind letter
from Miss Grant yesterday - Lady EW - well & always
kind

I had also a most kind letter
from Lord Carrington who tells me of two marriages
in his family that his second daughter Emily
is going to be married to Lord Granville
Somerset - son of the Duke of Beaufort
& his nephew to Lady Mary Anne Melville
sister to the Earl of - whose name
even I never knew before

So goes on
the world
marrying
and dying
and few
in it have
ever loved
one another
longer &
better th
as aunt
& niece
than
you & I
have my
dearest
aunt -
div.closer:
I am
your
truly
grateful
niece
&
friend
Maria E

postscript.p:
My mother
& aunt
Mary
& all the
young ones
send loves
